title: Magnetic-field control of interactions in alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms and applications to {\it XXZ} models
abstract: We study the magnetic-field dependence of the interactions between two alkaline-earth(-like) Rydberg atoms, ${}^{88}$Sr and ${}^{174}$Yb. Considering the pair of Rydberg states $|ns,{}^3S_1,m_J\rangle$ and $|(n+1)s,{}^3S_1,m_J\rangle$, we show that the effective Hamiltonian takes the form of an {\it XXZ}-type quantum spin model, as in the alkali-atom case [M. Kunimi and T. Tomita, Phys. Rev. A {\bf 112}, L051301 (2025)]. We find that the behavior of the anisotropy parameter for ${}^{174}$Yb at zero magnetic field is significantly different from that for other atomic species. This behavior originates from the strong spin-orbit coupling in ${}^{174}$Yb. We systematically calculate the interaction parameters of the {\it XXZ} model in the presence of a magnetic field and show that they can be tuned by the field. As applications to quantum many-body problems, we investigate one-dimensional systems in the large-anisotropy regime and show that the folded {\it XXZ} model can be realized in ${}^{174}$Yb systems without fine-tuning of the field. We also investigate two-dimensional square-lattice systems and show that a supersolid phase can emerge in the ground state at the mean-field level.
summary: This paper explores the use of magnetic fields to tune interactions in alkaline-earth Rydberg atoms. It highlights that 174Yb is particularly promising because its strong spin-orbit coupling leads to naturally large interaction anisotropy. The authors show that this allows for the simulation of complex many-body phenomena, such as the folded XXZ model in 1D and supersolid phases in 2D, with much greater ease than in traditional alkali-atom platforms.
main_problem: Investigating how magnetic fields can control the interactions between alkaline-earth-like Rydberg atoms to realize and tune XXZ-type quantum spin models.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that 174Yb exhibits significantly higher interaction anisotropy than 88Sr or alkali atoms due to strong spin-orbit coupling, allowing for the realization of large-anisotropy XXZ models and even supersolid phases without fine-tuning.
method: The study uses Multichannel Quantum Defect Theory (MQDT) and second-order perturbation theory to derive effective Hamiltonians and calculate interaction coefficients.
model_or_system: The system consists of alkaline-earth-like Rydberg atoms (88Sr and 174Yb) in an effective spin-1/2 XXZ-type quantum spin model derived from Rydberg state pairs.
key_observables: Anisotropy parameter (delta), C6 coefficients, Forster defects, and domain-wall number.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength (B), principal quantum number (n), and the anisotropy parameter (delta).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the validity of second-order perturbation theory and approximates the interaction using only the leading-order dipole-dipole term.
figures_summary: Figures show the dependence of anisotropy and C6 coefficients on the principal quantum number and magnetic field for both 88Sr and 174Yb.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem, derives the effective XXZ Hamiltonian using MQDT, compares the interaction properties of Sr and Yb, explores magnetic field tuning, and discusses applications to 1D and 2D many-body systems.
why_interesting: It provides a new platform (174Yb) for quantum simulation where complex spin models like the XXZ model can be realized with large anisotropy and high stability without the need for the precise fine-tuning required in alkali-atom systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Principal quantum number dependence of the anisotropy parameter δ in the absence of the magnetic field. (a) 88Sr. The green dotted and magenta dashed lines repre- sent δ = −1 and δ = 0, respectively. (b) 174Yb. The green dotted and magenta dashed lines represent δ = 0 and δ = 10, respectively.

FIG. 1. Principal quantum number dependence of the C6 parameters in the absence of the magnetic field for (a) 88Sr with |mJ| = 1, (b) 88Sr with mJ = 0, (c) 174Yb with |mJ| = 1, and (d) 174Yb with mJ = 0. The black solid line represents the n11 scaling as a guide to the eye.
FIG. 5. PR of the distribution of the Förster defects for (a) |mJ| = 1 and (b) mJ = 0. Here, ↑↑, ↑↓, and ↓↓represent the Förster defects ∆EF(n, ↑, ↑), ∆EF(n, ↑, ↓), and ∆EF(n, ↓, ↓), respectively.

FIG. 3. Principal-quantum-number dependence of the energy splittings of the intermediate P states. The energy splittings are evaluated as the maximum energy difference among the 3PJ states for 88Sr, and among the 3PJ and 1P1 states for 174Yb.

FIG. 4. Histogram of Förster defects for (a) 88Sr with |mJ| = 1, (b) a magnified view of panel (a), (c) 174Yb with |mJ| = 1, and (d) a magnified view of panel (c). Here, ↑↑, ↑↓, and ↓↓ represent the Förster defects ∆EF(n, ↑, ↑), ∆EF(n, ↑, ↓), and ∆EF(n, ↓, ↓), respectively. We count the intermediate states satisfying Rc > 0.1 nm to remove narrow resonances. The bin size is 0.1 GHz.

FIG. 6. Magnetic field and principal quantum number dependence of various physical quantities for 88Sr with mJ = +1 at θ = π/2. (a) C↑↑ 6 , (b) C↑↓ 6 , (c) C6, (d) R↑↑ c , (e) R↑↓ c , and (f) anisotropy parameter δ. The white region indicates the absence of data due to strong S–D mixing.

FIG. 7. Magnetic field and principal quantum number dependence of various physical quantities of 88Sr with mJ = 0 at θ = π/2. (a) C↑↑ 6 , (b) C↑↓ 6 , (c) C6, (d) R↑↑ c , (e) R↑↓ c , and (f) anisotropy parameter δ.

FIG. 8. Magnetic field and principal quantum number dependence of various physical quantities of 88Sr with mJ = −1 at θ = π/2. (a) C↑↑ 6 , (b) C↑↓ 6 , (c) C6, (d) R↑↑ c , (e) R↑↓ c , and (f) anisotropy parameter δ. The white region indicates the absence of data due to strong S–D mixing.

FIG. 9. Magnetic field and principal quantum number dependence of various physical quantities of 174Yb with mJ = +1 for θ = π/2. (a) C↑↑ 6 , (b) C↑↓ 6 , (c) C6, (d) R↑↑ c , (e) R↑↓ c , and (f) anisotropy parameter δ.

FIG. 10. Magnetic field and principal quantum number dependence of various physical quantities of 174Yb with mJ = 0 for θ = π/2. (a) C↑↑ 6 , (b) C↑↓ 6 , (c) C6, (d) R↑↑ c , (e) R↑↓ c , and (f) anisotropy parameter δ.
